title: Permissions Deep Dive
description: App-level runtime permissions, custom permission definitions, and system/OEM privileged permission infrastructure

references.title: Android Developers вҖ” Request Runtime Permissions
Android Developers вҖ” Define Custom Permissions

lesson:
Part 1: App Permissions (Basic / Intermediate)

Permission Types

Android groups permissions into four protection levels. The protection level determines how (and whether) the user is involved in granting:

Protection LevelGrant MechanismUser Prompt?Example
normalAuto-granted at installNoINTERNET, VIBRATE, BLUETOOTH
dangerousRuntime prompt (API 23+)YesCAMERA, READ_CONTACTS, ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION
signatureSame signing key as declaring appNoBIND_NOTIFICATION_LISTENER_SERVICE
specialMust redirect to SettingsYes (Settings UI)SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW, MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE
Key insight: normal permissions are declared in the manifest and silently granted вҖ” users never see a dialog. dangerous permissions require an explicit runtime request starting with API 23 (Marshmallow). Below API 23, all manifest permissions were granted at install time.

Declaring Permissions in AndroidManifest.xml

Two distinct tags serve different purposes:

<!-- REQUEST an existing permission (most common) --> <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.CAMERA" /> <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION" /> <!-- DEFINE a new custom permission (for your own apps/components) --> <permission android:name="com.example.myapp.OPEN_SETTINGS" android:label="Open App Settings" android:description="@string/perm_open_settings_desc" android:protectionLevel="signature" />
<uses-permission> = "My app needs this permission."
<permission> = "I am creating this permission for others to request."
Confusing these is a common mistake.

Runtime Permissions Flow (API 23+)

The canonical flow has four steps: check, rationale, request, handle.

App needs CAMERA в”Ӯ в–ј в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ checkSelfPermission() в”Ӯ в”Ӯ == PERMISSION_GRANTED? в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ YES в”Ӯ NO в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в–ј в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ shouldShowRequestPermission в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Rationale() == true? в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ YES в”Ӯ NO в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в–ј в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Show rationale UI в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (explain WHY) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в–ј в–ј в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ requestPermissions() в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ system dialog appears в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в–ј в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ onRequestPermissionsResult в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ GRANTED or DENIED в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в–ј в–ј Use feature Degrade gracefully

Modern API: ActivityResultContracts

The old requestPermissions() / onRequestPermissionsResult() pattern is deprecated. Use registerForActivityResult() with the permission contracts:

// Single permission val cameraLauncher = registerForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() ) { isGranted: Boolean -> if (isGranted) { openCamera() } else { showPermissionDeniedUI() } } // Usage: launch with the permission string cameraLauncher.launch(Manifest.permission.CAMERA) // Multiple permissions at once val multiLauncher = registerForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.RequestMultiplePermissions() ) { permissions: Map<String, Boolean> -> val cameraGranted = permissions[Manifest.permission.CAMERA] ?: false val micGranted = permissions[Manifest.permission.RECORD_AUDIO] ?: false when { cameraGranted && micGranted -> startVideoRecording() cameraGranted -> startPhotoCapture() // degrade: no mic else -> showDeniedState() } } multiLauncher.launch(arrayOf( Manifest.permission.CAMERA, Manifest.permission.RECORD_AUDIO ))

Compose: rememberLauncherForActivityResult

@Composable fun CameraScreen() { val context = LocalContext.current var hasPermission by remember { mutableStateOf( ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission( context, Manifest.permission.CAMERA ) == PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED ) } val launcher = rememberLauncherForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() ) { granted -> hasPermission = granted } if (hasPermission) { CameraPreview() } else { Button(onClick = { launcher.launch(Manifest.permission.CAMERA) }) { Text("Grant Camera Access") } } }

shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale вҖ” The Tricky State Machine

This method returns different values depending on the user's history with the permission. Understanding it is critical for handling all edge cases:

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale() в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ Scenario в”Ӯ Returns в”Ӯ What to do в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ Never asked before в”Ӯ false в”Ӯ Just request it в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Denied once (no checkbox) в”Ӯ true в”Ӯ Show rationale, then в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ request again в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Denied + "Don't ask again" в”Ӯ false в”Ӯ Cannot request again! в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (or denied twice on 11+) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Redirect to Settings в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Already granted в”Ӯ false в”Ӯ Use the permission в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ State Transitions: в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ request() в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ request() в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ VIRGIN в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ в”Ӯ DENIED ONCE в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ в”Ӯ PERMANENTLY в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (false) в”Ӯ deny в”Ӯ (true) в”Ӯ deny+check в”Ӯ DENIED (false) в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ grant в”Ӯ grant в”Ӯ user goes to в–ј в–ј в”Ӯ Settings в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в–ј в”Ӯ GRANTED в”Ӯ в”Ӯ GRANTED в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ (false) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (false) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ GRANTED в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
The "Don't ask again" trap: Once the user checks "Don't ask again" (or on Android 11+ after two denials), shouldShowRationale returns false AND calling requestPermissions() immediately returns DENIED without showing a dialog. The ONLY way to recover is to send the user to app Settings with ACTION_APPLICATION_DETAILS_SETTINGS.
// Detecting the "permanently denied" state val launcher = registerForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() ) { isGranted -> if (!isGranted) { // After denial, check rationale again val shouldShowRationale = shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale( Manifest.permission.CAMERA ) if (!shouldShowRationale) { // "Don't ask again" was checked вҖ” redirect to Settings val intent = Intent(Settings.ACTION_APPLICATION_DETAILS_SETTINGS).apply { data = Uri.fromParts("package", packageName, null) } startActivity(intent) } } }

Permission Groups

Dangerous permissions belong to groups (e.g., LOCATION group contains ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION and ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION). Historically, granting one permission in a group auto-granted the rest. Since Android 12 (API 31), each permission must be requested individually.

Never rely on group grants. Google can change group membership or behavior across versions. Always check and request each specific permission you need.

Special Permissions

Some permissions cannot be granted via the standard runtime dialog. You must redirect the user to the system Settings app:

// SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW вҖ” draw over other apps if (!Settings.canDrawOverlays(context)) { val intent = Intent( Settings.ACTION_MANAGE_OVERLAY_PERMISSION, Uri.parse("package:${context.packageName}") ) startActivity(intent) } // MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE (API 30+) вҖ” access all files if (!Environment.isExternalStorageManager()) { val intent = Intent(Settings.ACTION_MANAGE_ALL_FILES_ACCESS_PERMISSION) startActivity(intent) } // REQUEST_INSTALL_PACKAGES вҖ” sideload APKs if (!packageManager.canRequestPackageInstalls()) { val intent = Intent( Settings.ACTION_MANAGE_UNKNOWN_APP_SOURCES, Uri.parse("package:${context.packageName}") ) startActivity(intent) }

Version-Specific Permission Changes

APIChangeImpact
23 (M)Runtime permissions introducedAll dangerous permissions require runtime request
26 (O)Background location limitsBackground apps cannot access location continuously
29 (Q)ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATIONSeparate permission required for background location; must request it alone, not with foreground location
30 (R)One-time permissions, auto-resetCamera/mic/location can be granted once; unused apps lose permissions
31 (S)Camera/mic indicators, BLUETOOTH_CONNECTGreen dot in status bar; Bluetooth requires new runtime permissions
33 (T)POST_NOTIFICATIONSNotifications now require runtime permission
34 (U)Photo/video partial accessUsers can grant access to selected photos only
Background location (API 29+): You must request ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION or ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION first, get it granted, then request ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATION separately. Requesting them together causes the system to ignore the background request.

Best Practices

Permission Request Best Practices:
1. Ask in context вҖ” request when the user taps the camera button, not at app launch
2. Explain before asking вҖ” show a rationale UI before the system dialog
3. Handle denial gracefully вҖ” never crash or show a blank screen
4. Degrade features вҖ” if location is denied, still show manual city entry
5. Minimize permissions вҖ” only request what you actually need
6. Use coarse before fine вҖ” on API 31+, let users choose approximate location first
7. Never ask repeatedly in a loop вҖ” respect the user's decision

Part 2: Defining Custom Permissions

Creating a Custom Permission

You define a custom permission when you want to restrict access to your app's exported components. Any app that wants to interact with your component must hold this permission.

<!-- In the DECLARING app's manifest (the one that owns the component) --> <manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"> <!-- Define the permission --> <permission android:name="com.example.provider.READ_DATA" android:label="Read Provider Data" android:description="@string/perm_read_data_desc" android:protectionLevel="signature" /> <!-- Enforce it on a ContentProvider --> <provider android:name=".MyContentProvider" android:authorities="com.example.provider" android:readPermission="com.example.provider.READ_DATA" android:writePermission="com.example.provider.WRITE_DATA" android:exported="true" /> </manifest> <!-- In the REQUESTING app's manifest (the client) --> <uses-permission android:name="com.example.provider.READ_DATA" />

protectionLevel Values

protectionLevelMeaningWho Can Get It
normalLow-risk, auto-grantedAny app that declares <uses-permission>
dangerousRuntime promptAny app, but user must approve
signatureSame signing certificateOnly apps signed with the SAME key as the declaring app
signatureOrSystemDeprecated (API 23)Same key OR pre-installed system app. Use signature|privileged instead
signature|privilegedSignature OR privilegedSame key, or a privileged system app allowlisted in privapp-permissions.xml
internalPlatform-internal (API 28+)Only requestable by system processes or apps on the system image
Signature permissions are the workhorse for inter-app trust between apps you control. If you publish App A and App B signed with the same key, App B can use App A's signature-level components without any user prompt. This is how OEMs link their pre-installed apps.

Enforcing Permissions on Components

<!-- Activity: only apps holding MY_PERMISSION can launch this --> <activity android:name=".AdminActivity" android:permission="com.example.MY_PERMISSION" android:exported="true" /> <!-- Service: only permitted apps can bind --> <service android:name=".SyncService" android:permission="com.example.SYNC_PERMISSION" android:exported="true" /> <!-- BroadcastReceiver: only permitted apps' broadcasts are delivered --> <receiver android:name=".SecureReceiver" android:permission="com.example.SEND_UPDATES" android:exported="true"> <intent-filter> <action android:name="com.example.ACTION_UPDATE" /> </intent-filter> </receiver> <!-- ContentProvider: separate read/write permissions --> <provider android:name=".DataProvider" android:authorities="com.example.data" android:readPermission="com.example.READ" android:writePermission="com.example.WRITE" android:exported="true" />

Part 3: System / OEM Permissions (Advanced)

Privileged App Permissions (priv-app)

Android distinguishes between regular pre-installed apps (/system/app/) and privileged pre-installed apps (/system/priv-app/ or /product/priv-app/). Privileged apps can be granted permissions with signature|privileged protection level вҖ” even without sharing the platform signing key.

Android System Partitions & App Locations: /system/ в”ңв”Җв”Җ app/ вҶҗ Regular pre-installed apps (no special privileges) в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җ Calculator/ в”ңв”Җв”Җ priv-app/ вҶҗ Privileged system apps (can get privileged permissions) в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җ Settings/ в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җ SystemUI/ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җ Launcher3/ в”ңв”Җв”Җ etc/ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җ permissions/ вҶҗ Permission allowlist XMLs в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җ privapp-permissions-platform.xml в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җ com.example.oem.xml в””в”Җв”Җ framework/ /product/ в”ңв”Җв”Җ priv-app/ вҶҗ OEM privileged apps (same privileges as /system/priv-app) в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җ OEMSettings/ в””в”Җв”Җ etc/ в””в”Җв”Җ permissions/ в””в”Җв”Җ privapp-permissions-product.xml /vendor/ в”ңв”Җв”Җ priv-app/ вҶҗ Vendor-specific privileged apps в””в”Җв”Җ etc/ в””в”Җв”Җ permissions/ в””в”Җв”Җ privapp-permissions-vendor.xml

privapp-permissions.xml

Even if an app is in /system/priv-app/, it still must be explicitly allowlisted in a privapp-permissions.xml file to receive privileged permissions. This was introduced in Android 8.0 (Oreo) to reduce the attack surface of pre-installed apps.

<!-- /etc/permissions/privapp-permissions-platform.xml --> <permissions> <!-- Grant specific privileged permissions to Settings app --> <privapp-permissions package="com.android.settings"> <permission name="android.permission.WRITE_SECURE_SETTINGS" /> <permission name="android.permission.MANAGE_USERS" /> <permission name="android.permission.STATUS_BAR_SERVICE" /> <permission name="android.permission.CHANGE_CONFIGURATION" /> </privapp-permissions> <!-- SystemUI gets a different set --> <privapp-permissions package="com.android.systemui"> <permission name="android.permission.STATUS_BAR" /> <permission name="android.permission.BIND_NOTIFICATION_LISTENER_SERVICE" /> <permission name="android.permission.MEDIA_CONTENT_CONTROL" /> </privapp-permissions> <!-- Explicitly DENY a permission to a priv-app --> <privapp-permissions package="com.example.bloatware"> <deny-permission name="android.permission.READ_CALL_LOG" /> </privapp-permissions> </permissions>
Enforcement: Starting with Android 9 (Pie), the system crashes on boot if a privileged app tries to use a permission that is not allowlisted. During development, you can set ro.control_privapp_permissions=log to log violations instead of crashing, but production builds enforce strictly.

default-permissions.xml

This file pre-grants runtime (dangerous) permissions to specified apps without requiring user interaction. OEMs use it to ensure their pre-installed camera, dialer, and messaging apps work out of the box.

<!-- /etc/default-permissions/default-permissions-oem.xml --> <exceptions> <!-- Pre-grant camera and storage to the OEM camera app --> <exception package="com.oem.camera"> <permission name="android.permission.CAMERA" fixed="true" /> <permission name="android.permission.RECORD_AUDIO" fixed="true" /> <permission name="android.permission.READ_MEDIA_IMAGES" fixed="true" /> </exception> <!-- Pre-grant phone permissions to the dialer --> <exception package="com.oem.dialer"> <permission name="android.permission.CALL_PHONE" fixed="true" /> <permission name="android.permission.READ_CALL_LOG" fixed="true" /> <permission name="android.permission.READ_CONTACTS" fixed="true" /> </exception> </exceptions>
fixed="true" means the user cannot revoke the permission from Settings. Without fixed, the user can revoke it after first boot вҖ” the pre-grant just skips the initial prompt.

Platform Signing

Apps signed with the platform key (the key used to sign the Android system image itself) automatically receive all permissions with signature protection level defined by the platform. This is the highest level of trust in Android:

Permission Grant Hierarchy: в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ Platform-signed apps (signed with the platform .pk8/.x509) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Get ALL signature-level platform permissions automatically в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Can access ANY system API в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Examples: Settings, SystemUI, Phone в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ Privileged apps (/system/priv-app or /product/priv-app) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Get signature|privileged permissions IF allowlisted в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Cannot get pure signature permissions (need platform key) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Examples: OEM Launcher, pre-installed apps в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ System apps (/system/app) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Pre-installed but no extra privileges в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Can get default-permissions pre-grants for runtime perms в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Cannot get privileged or signature permissions в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ Third-party apps (/data/app) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Standard runtime permission model only в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ No access to privileged or signature permissions в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Vendor / OEM Custom Permissions

OEMs can define their own permission hierarchy to control access to proprietary features (custom hardware APIs, device management, enterprise features):

<!-- OEM defines a custom permission in their framework overlay --> <!-- frameworks/base/core/res/AndroidManifest.xml (OEM fork) --> <permission android:name="com.oem.permission.ACCESS_CUSTOM_SENSOR" android:protectionLevel="signature|privileged" android:label="Access Custom Sensor" /> <!-- OEM's HAL service enforces the permission --> <!-- Only OEM's own apps (signed with OEM key) or allowlisted --> <!-- priv-apps can use this sensor --> <!-- Allowlist in /vendor/etc/permissions/privapp-permissions-oem.xml --> <permissions> <privapp-permissions package="com.oem.sensorapp"> <permission name="com.oem.permission.ACCESS_CUSTOM_SENSOR" /> </privapp-permissions> </permissions>

SELinux and Permissions

Android's permission system (DAC вҖ” Discretionary Access Control) is only one layer. SELinux adds Mandatory Access Control (MAC) вҖ” a kernel-level enforcement that even root cannot bypass:

Two-Layer Security Model: в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ Layer 1: Android Permissions (DAC) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ "Does the app have android.permission.CAMERA?" в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Enforced by system_server (PackageManagerService) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Apps can check/request at runtime в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ User-visible permission dialogs в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө в”Ӯ Layer 2: SELinux (MAC) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ "Is this process ALLOWED to open /dev/video0?" в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Enforced by the Linux kernel в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Based on security labels, NOT user identity в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Cannot be overridden by the app or even root в”Ӯ в”Ӯ вҶ’ Defined in .te policy files compiled into the system в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ BOTH must allow access. Having the Android permission is necessary but not sufficient вҖ” the SELinux policy must also grant access to the underlying resource. Example: Camera access requires: 1. android.permission.CAMERA (Android permission check) 2. SELinux allow rule: (Kernel-level check) allow untrusted_app camera_device:chr_file { read write };
Why this matters for OEM development: When adding a new hardware feature or system service, you must:
1. Define an Android permission in the manifest
2. Write SELinux policy (.te files) granting the appropriate process domains access to the device nodes or service labels
3. Allowlist any privileged apps that need the permission
Forgetting the SELinux policy is the #1 reason a permission-granted feature still returns "Permission denied" in logcat вҖ” look for avc: denied in dmesg.

Certificate-Based Permission Allowlisting (knownSigner)

Starting with API 34 (Android 14), Android supports knownSigner вҖ” a declarative way to grant permissions to apps based on their signing certificate hash. No runtime code needed.

The Modern Pattern: signature|knownSigner

<!-- Define permission granted to specific signing certs --> <permission android:name="com.example.permission.BIND_SERVICE" android:protectionLevel="signature|knownSigner" android:knownCerts="@array/allowed_certs" /> <!-- res/values/known_certs.xml --> <resources> <string-array name="allowed_certs"> <item>A1B2C3D4E5F6...</item> <!-- Partner app cert SHA-256 --> <item>E5F6G7H8I9J0...</item> <!-- Internal app cert --> </string-array> </resources> <!-- Service protected by this permission вҖ” system-enforced! --> <service android:name=".MyPrivilegedService" android:permission="com.example.permission.BIND_SERVICE" android:exported="true" />
How it works: When an app tries to bind, PackageManager checks the caller's signing cert hash against the knownCerts array. If it matches вҶ’ permission granted. If not вҶ’ SecurityException. No runtime code, no PackageValidator class. The system handles everything before your code even runs.

Why Certificate Hash, Not Package Name?

Package names are spoofable вҖ” any app can declare any package name. Signing certificates are unforgeable. The SHA-256 hash of the certificate is the true identity of an app.

Pre-API-34: Runtime PackageValidator Pattern

Before knownSigner, you had to check certificates in code:

// Old pattern: manual cert check in onBind() override fun onBind(intent: Intent): IBinder? { val callerPkg = callingPackage ?: return null val info = packageManager.getPackageInfo( callerPkg, PackageManager.GET_SIGNING_CERTIFICATES ) val certHash = sha256(info.signingInfo.apkContentsSigners[0].toByteArray()) // Check against allowlist (often loaded from XML resource) if (certHash !in ALLOWED_CERT_HASHES) { throw SecurityException("Unauthorized caller: $callerPkg") } return binder }
The old pattern is error-prone: bugs in your verification logic can be exploited. knownSigner moves enforcement to the system level where it can't be bypassed by app code.
EraPatternEnforcement
Oldsignature onlySame signing key вҖ” too restrictive for partner apps
MidRuntime PackageValidatorCode checks cert hash in onBind() вҖ” flexible but manual
Current (API 34+)signature|knownSignerSystem-enforced via XML cert hashes вҖ” declarative, unforgeable

questions.title: Permission Types
questions.description: Which permission type requires the user to explicitly approve at runtime via a system dialog?
options: normal вҖ” all permissions show a dialog since Android 6.0
dangerous вҖ” requires a runtime prompt on API 23+
signature вҖ” prompts only if the signing keys differ
special вҖ” requires a dialog managed by the app itself
questions.explanation: dangerous permissions are the only type that triggers the standard runtime permission dialog. normal permissions are auto-granted at install. signature permissions are silently granted if the requesting app shares the same signing key. special permissions (like SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW) require a redirect to Settings, not a standard dialog.
hints: Think about what changed in Android 6.0 (Marshmallow, API 23)

questions.title: shouldShowRationale States
questions.description: A user opens your app for the first time. You have never requested CAMERA permission. What does shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale(CAMERA) return?
options: true вҖ” you should always show a rationale before the first request
false вҖ” the user has never denied it, so no rationale is needed yet
Throws an exception because the permission has not been requested
true on Android 11+, false on older versions
questions.explanation: shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale() returns false when the permission has never been requested. It only returns true after the user has denied the permission at least once (without checking "Don't ask again"). This is unintuitive because false can mean three things: never asked, already granted, or permanently denied.
hints: This method returns false in MORE cases than you might expect

questions.title: The "Don't Ask Again" Edge Case
questions.description: The user has denied CAMERA permission twice on Android 11 (which auto-applies "Don't ask again" after two denials). What happens when this code runs?
options: The system shows the permission dialog normally
The callback fires immediately with isGranted = false, no dialog is shown
The system redirects to Settings automatically
The app crashes with a SecurityException
questions.explanation: Once a permission is permanently denied (user checked "Don't ask again" or denied twice on Android 11+), calling launch() or requestPermissions() does NOT show any dialog. The callback fires immediately with false. The app must detect this state (using shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale returning false after a denial) and redirect the user to Settings.ACTION_APPLICATION_DETAILS_SETTINGS instead.
hints: Think about what "Don't ask again" means literally вҖ” the system will NOT ask again
The callback still fires, but the user never sees a dialog

questions.title: protectionLevel Differences
questions.description: Your company publishes two apps: App A (defines a signature-level permission) and App B (requests it). Both are signed with the same company key and installed from the Play Store. What happens?
options: App B gets the permission automatically вҖ” no user prompt, no Settings redirect
App B must request the permission at runtime like a dangerous permission
The user must approve both apps in Settings
This only works for pre-installed system apps, not Play Store apps
questions.explanation: signature-level permissions are granted automatically and silently when the requesting app shares the same signing certificate as the app that defined the permission. No user interaction required. This works for any app вҖ” Play Store or pre-installed. It's the mechanism for establishing trust between your own apps without involving the user.
hints: The whole point of signature permissions is trust between apps from the same developer

questions.title: privapp-permissions.xml Purpose
questions.description: An OEM places their custom app in /system/priv-app/. The app requests android.permission.WRITE_SECURE_SETTINGS (a signature|privileged permission). What happens if the permission is NOT listed in any privapp-permissions.xml?
options: The permission is granted anyway because the app is in priv-app
The permission is silently denied at runtime
The system logs a warning but still grants the permission
On Android 9+, the device fails to boot (or the app is disabled)
questions.explanation: Starting with Android 9 (Pie), the system strictly enforces the privapp-permissions allowlist. If a privileged app requests a signature|privileged permission that is NOT in any privapp-permissions.xml file, the system will crash during boot in production builds. During development, setting ro.control_privapp_permissions=log downgrades this to a logcat warning. Being in /system/priv-app/ is necessary but not sufficient вҖ” you MUST be allowlisted.
hints: Android 9 introduced strict enforcement for security
Think about what happens at boot time, not at runtime

questions.title: Runtime Permission Check
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to check if camera permission is granted and request it if not.

blanks.hint: The AndroidX compat class for permission checks
The constant representing a granted permission

questions.explanation: ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission() is the recommended way to check permissions вҖ” it handles API-level differences internally. The result is compared against PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED (value 0). If not granted, launch the permission request. Always check before using a protected API.
hints: Use the AndroidX compat wrapper, not the raw Context method
The result constant lives in PackageManager

questions.title: SELinux vs Android Permissions
questions.description: Explain the relationship between Android permissions and SELinux. Why might an app that holds android.permission.CAMERA still get a "Permission denied" error?
questions.answer: Android has two layers of access control: Android permissions (DAC вҖ” Discretionary Access Control) checked by system_server, and SELinux (MAC вҖ” Mandatory Access Control) enforced by the Linux kernel. Both must allow access. An app can hold android.permission.CAMERA and still fail if the SELinux policy does not include an 'allow' rule for that app's security domain to access the camera device node. Look for 'avc: denied' in dmesg to diagnose. This is especially common when OEMs add new hardware features and forget to write the corresponding .te policy rules.
questions.explanation: SELinux operates independently from Android's permission framework. Even root processes can be blocked by SELinux. When debugging permission issues on system/OEM apps, always check both layers: logcat for Android permission denials and dmesg for SELinux (AVC) denials.
hints: Android has TWO independent security layers
One is checked by system_server, one is checked by the kernel
What log message would you look for if SELinux blocked something?

questions.title: Background Location Request
questions.description: This code tries to request foreground and background location together. On Android 11+ it fails silently вҖ” background location is never granted. Fix it.

checks.message: Request background location separately after foreground is granted

questions.explanation: On Android 11+ (API 30), requesting ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATION together with foreground location causes the system to silently ignore the background request. You must first obtain foreground location, then request background location in a separate request. The background request redirects the user to a Settings-like page where they choose "Allow all the time."
hints: Android 11 changed how background location requests work
Think about the order: which permission must be granted FIRST?

questions.title: default-permissions.xml
questions.description: What is the purpose of default-permissions.xml on an Android system image?
options: It defines which permissions exist in the system
It pre-grants runtime (dangerous) permissions to specified apps without user interaction
It replaces privapp-permissions.xml on Android 12+
It configures the default permission dialog appearance
questions.explanation: default-permissions.xml (located in /etc/default-permissions/) allows OEMs to pre-grant runtime permissions to pre-installed apps so they work out of the box. For example, the OEM Camera app gets CAMERA and RECORD_AUDIO pre-granted. With fixed="true", the user cannot even revoke these permissions from Settings. This is separate from privapp-permissions.xml, which handles privileged (not runtime) permissions.
hints: Think about what an OEM needs for their pre-installed Camera app
This is about runtime/dangerous permissions, not privileged permissions

questions.title: Component Permission Enforcement
questions.description: App A defines and exports a ContentProvider with this manifest entry. App B (signed with a different key) tries to query it. What happens?
questions.code:
<!-- App A's manifest -->
<permission
    android:name="com.a.READ_SECRETS"
    android:protectionLevel="signature" />

<provider
    android:name=".SecretsProvider"
    android:authorities="com.a.secrets"
    android:readPermission="com.a.READ_SECRETS"
    android:exported="true" />

<!-- App B's manifest -->
<uses-permission android:name="com.a.READ_SECRETS" />
options: App B can read the provider because it declared <uses-permission>
App B gets a SecurityException вҖ” different signing key means signature permission is denied
App B is prompted at runtime to approve the permission
The system ignores the readPermission attribute because the provider is exported
questions.explanation: A signature-level permission is only granted to apps signed with the same certificate as the app that defined the permission. Declaring <uses-permission> is necessary but not sufficient вҖ” the signing key must match. App B, signed with a different key, will receive a SecurityException when attempting to query the ContentProvider. This is by design вҖ” signature permissions create a trust boundary between apps from the same developer.
hints: What does 'signature' protection level actually check?
Declaring <uses-permission> alone is not enough for signature-level permissions
